sub_issue: Applying for or obtaining a modification, forbearance plan, short sale, or deed-in-lieu
complaint_what_happened:
I. CHRONOLOGICAL TIMELINE OF CONTRADICTORY COMMUNICATIONS XX/XX/XXXX Acknowledgement of Complete Package The Servicer acknowledged receipt of a complete loss mitigation application package. At this point, the Servicer represented that all required documentation had been received and the application was under review. 
XX/XX/XXXX Two Contradictory Letters on Same Day The Servicer mailed TWO separate letters dated XX/XX/XXXX, each titled " Request for additional documentation. '' These letters, sent on the same day, contained differing content while simultaneously reversing the prior acknowledgement of completeness. The package previously declared complete was now, inexplicably, incomplete. 
Both letters demanded production of a document that does not exist : " Document : Contact your Relationship Manager '' " Contact your Relationship Manager '' is not a document. It is a demand for verbal communication. This demand serves no legitimate purpose other than to avoid creating written records of the Servicer 's representations and to manufacture grounds for application denial by requesting production of something that can not be produced. 
XX/XX/XXXX Notice of " New '' Relationship Manager The Servicer sent a notice purporting to announce a new Relationship Manager assignment. The named individual was XXXX XXXX same Relationship Manager previously assigned. No actual change had occurred. This letter served only to reset procedural timelines and create the false appearance of responsive action. 
XX/XX/XXXX First Legitimate Document Request For the first time since XX/XX/XXXX, the Servicer requested an actual, producible document. This document was signed and submitted within the stated deadline, in full compliance with the Servicer 's instructions. 
XX/XX/XXXX Assistance Review Cancellation Despite timely submission of all legitimately requested documentation, the Servicer cancelled the assistance review. No explanation was provided for how an application could be complete on XX/XX/XXXX, incomplete on XX/XX/XXXX due to a non-existent " document, '' cured by XX/XX/XXXX submission, and then cancelled on XX/XX/XXXX. 
XXXX. QUALIFIED WRITTEN REQUEST AND INADEQUATE RESPONSE XX/XX/XXXX Qualified Written Request via Certified Mail I submitted a Qualified Written Request pursuant to 12 U.S.C. 2605 ( e ), sent via certified mail with return receipt requested. The QWR requested, inter alia : complete chain of title documentation, proof of transfer of the Note, the XXXX XXXX XXXX pool number in which my loan is held, the servicing agreement between U.S. Bank and XXXX XXXX, and all records maintained regarding my account. 
Servicer 's Inadequate Response The Servicer 's response was materially deficient under RESPA. Rather than providing the requested documentation, the Servicer : 1. Declared that details involving my loan and a federal government entity ( XXXX XXXX ) constitute " confidential information. '' XXXX. Failed to provide anything beyond my payment recordinformation I already possess. 
XXXX. Enclosed copies of the XXXX contradictory XX/XX/XXXX lettersdocuments I had already received and which form the basis of this complaint. 
The claim that information about MY loan is " confidential '' from ME is legally indefensible. XXXX XXXX is a wholly-owned government corporation within HUD, subject to the Freedom of Information Act ( 5 U.S.C. 552 ). I am entitled under the Privacy Act ( 5 U.S.C. 552a ) to all records maintained by federal agencies pertaining to my individual account. The Servicer can not invoke " confidentiality '' to shield basic loan documentation from the borrower.

III. THE SERVICER 'S CONSCIENTIOUS WARNING I would be remiss if I failed to note that among the Servicer 's correspondence was a thoughtfully included pamphlet titled " Beware of Foreclosure Scams. '' The pamphlet warns consumers against entities that : Demand payment for services that provide no benefit Request documentation that can not be produced Create confusion through contradictory communications Obstruct legitimate loss mitigation review The irony is not lost on me.

IV. SPECIFIC RESPA AND REGULATION X VIOLATIONS 12 C.F.R. 1024.41 ( b ) ( 2 ) ( i ) ( B ) Failure to exercise reasonable diligence in obtaining documents. Requesting a non-existent " document '' ( " Contact your Relationship Manager '' ) does not constitute reasonable diligence.

12 C.F.R. 1024.41 ( c ) ( 1 ) Failure to provide accurate information about loss mitigation options. Sending contradictory letters on the same day regarding application status violates the requirement to provide accurate information.

12 U.S.C. 2605 ( e ) Failure to respond adequately to Qualified Written Request. Claiming borrower 's own loan documentation is " confidential '' does not constitute a compliant response.

12 C.F.R. 1024.41 ( g ) Bad faith denial of loss mitigation. Cancelling an application after manufacturing incompleteness through impossible documentation demands constitutes procedural bad faith. 
V. RELIEF REQUESTED XXXX. Reinstatement of loss mitigation review based on the XX/XX/XXXX complete application. 
XXXX. Complete response to my XX/XX/XXXX Qualified Written Request, including all documentation previously withheld as " confidential. '' XXXX. Written explanation of how my application transitioned from complete to incomplete based on a demand for a non-existent document. 
XXXX. Identification of the XXXX XXXX trust pool number and production of the servicing agreement. 
XXXX. Statutory damages under RESPA for XXXX violations. 
XXXX. Cease and desist from further demands for documentation that does not exist. 
VI. CONCLUSION The Servicer has demonstrated a pattern of conduct designed to obstruct loss mitigation review through procedural gamesmanship : acknowledging completeness, then reversing that acknowledgement based on impossible demands, then cancelling the review when manufactured deficiencies could not be cured. This is not inadvertent error. This is systematic obstruction. 
I respectfully request that the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau investigate these practices and take appropriate enforcement action. 
This complaint, and all responses or non-responses thereto, will be preserved as evidence for potential civil litigation including claims under RESPA and the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act.

issue: Applying for a mortgage or refinancing an existing mortgage
complaint_what_happened:
In XXXX of XXXX I requested an application be started for a HELOC through the company that bought my initial mortgage, Pennymac. I knew I was on the low but acceptable range of XXXX XXXX ( XXXX ), and had the equity in my house to pull from. I paid the application fee ( $ XXXX {$460.00} ) ( was told they would guide me through the process ), and then the agent immediately spent 2 straight days pushing me to do a cash out refinance at much worse terms, trying to convince me that I wouldn't be eligible, for several reasons. In fact, it was mostly over the fact that she said I needed to use the entirety of the remaining loan to pay off my car, and it would make more sense to do a cash out refinance at the worse rate ( not true, this really doesn't factor in the way CC debt does, and that's how she categorized my car loan for some reason ). I continued through the process and was constantly re-requested of things I had already submitted to their processing team, which, after painfully pointing these things they needed out on the documents, they were accepted. I would get nearly a half hour call a day, if not 2, to ask me to correct the information, and provide x thing, which I would reiterate that was already submitted. They would open it, and deny it within the day, not looking at what I would send in. They then had the appraisal done, and with the appraisal coming in XXXX short of the estimated market value ( 1 bed 1 bath ), I asked to do a reconsideration of value. I then responded to the email with my 3 comps ( within 15 minutes of my house as there aren't many lake neighborhood houses on double lots with no rear neighbors due to a forest preserve and access to the private lake ), and asked if there could be a reconsideration. I sent these in to my loan officer, received no word back. I called, was given no call back, just an email. Received an email about documents to sign that contained the denial letter. I called my loan officer, left a message on the number in her signature, and let her know I didn't hear back from her the whole last week and I felt I was denied my right to an ROV through this process. I even found the house that they comped it to. It was literally sold a month before for the same value as they gave mine, and it is in a much worse area, with worse schools, no lake, no side lot and no comparison to our fit and finish.

I was still pretty upset about this and looked into making a complaint with the CFPB and I was told to try and mediate this with them. Which is fine. I respect that. I took this to the attention of a young woman ( XX/XX/XXXX @ XXXX ), who I believe said she was in TN, and was tracking down my LO and her branch manager for me. Branch manager said she was free for a chat and then ignored my call. Called me back 20 minutes later ( XXXX ) and essentially told me I was insane, and that her LO wouldn't take a knee on my ROV to push me into the cash out refinance. She hands me off to a Tier 3 Manager of Client Relations, who, seemingly did not get the whole story before calling me and emailing me the following Monday, 3/31. She was essentially talking like they had a weird sticky situation with an in process loan, rather than addressing a complaint. She insisted I provide the ROV emails that went unanswered and stated that she can not find any record of me ever leaving a voicemail for my LO after the denial, where I requested to file a complaint. I sent these the unanswered email chain about the ROV 3/31 at 7pm, during my dinner, to the T3 CR manager. She then did not respond or call me at all for 2 weeks. I sent an email looking for follow up last Friday ( 4/11 ), and received an email today, 4/14 saying that they couldn't use the comps I provided them, and too bad, so sad, we wont reconsider. And that wasn't really the point, though, as I had intended to get my money back for the terrible service on my loan application ( what they literally told me I paid for ) and submit a complaint. She insisted when I contacted her by phone today, that there wasn't anything wrong with the fact I wasn't told the comps wouldn't work. She didn't seem to think the 2 week wait from when she saw that email chain with the LO go unanswered, to today, was a problem, either. She said they would rather use average home value sales for similar homes, than comps, which again, stacks the deck against a very rare find in real estate, and I think, between my low loan value, income, and general age, I was discriminated against. I think they saw someone who they could force into a terrible cash out refinance, and pounded that idea until the point it normally works for them with other people my age. They have made it clear, that if you have any kind of unique features to your house, know they don't care. complaint_what_happened:
We, XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX XXXX, are writing to formally appeal the denial of our application for home retention loss mitigation options as outlined in your letter dated XX/XX/XXXX. We believe that SPSs determination was incorrect due to procedural errors, misapplication of eligibility criteria, and failure to fully consider applicable legal protections under federal and New York State law. We request a reconsideration of our application for the SPS Trial Modification, SPS Unemployment Program, Deferral Plan, and Repayment Plan. Below, we outline the basis for our appeal, supported by evidence and legal arguments. 
Background and Request for Reconsideration Our property at XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, XXXX, NY XXXX, is our family residence, and we are committed to retaining it. Your letter indicates that our mortgage obligation is either discharged or subject to an automatic stay under the United States Bankruptcy Code, yet the lien remains enforceable. We acknowledge the accounts delinquency and bankruptcy status but assert that SPSs review failed to adequately account for our circumstances, applicable investor guidelines, and regulatory requirements.

Legal Basis for Appeal 1. Violation of Federal Servicing Regulations ( Regulation X, 12 CFR 1024.41 ) Under the Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act ( RESPA ), implemented through Regulation X, mortgage servicers must exercise reasonable diligence in obtaining documents and information to complete a loss mitigation application and evaluate all available options ( 12 CFR 1024.41 ( b ) ( 1 ) ). Your denial letter lists specific reasons for non-approval but provides insufficient detail on how these conclusions were reached. For example : o SPS Trial Modification ( Insufficient Payment Reduction ) : The letter states that a modification could not reduce our principal and interest payment sufficiently. However, no Net Present Value ( NPV ) calculation specificsbeyond a property value of $ XXXXare provided to justify this finding. Regulation X requires servicers to provide borrowers with a description of the inputs used in the NPV calculation ( 12 CFR 1024.41 ( d ) ( 2 ) ( ii ) ). The incomplete NPV data in your letter suggests a lack of transparency and potentially an error in underwriting. We request the full NPV inputs and methodology to verify compliance with investor guidelines ( e.g., XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, or other applicable standards ). 
o SPS Unemployment Program and Deferral Plan ( Account Delinquency ) : The blanket denial based on account delinquency fails to consider whether partial payment deferrals or forbearance could cure the delinquency, as permitted under federal guidelines.

In XX/XX/year> we were contacted by our mortgage adviser at Chase bank and informed we were eligible for a mortgage rate reduction of XXXX percentage points off the current rates. We were encouraged to seize on the opportunity. 

After repeatedly asking if there was a " catch, '' we were assured there was none. We simply had XXXX months to get under contract. Our adviser, in discussion our loan options, encouraged us to keep our current home and the Chase mortgage we have on it. We locked our rate. 

We proceeded with a home search, and in early XXXX went under contract for a new home. All of the documents we received and signed, and all of our communications with our Chase mortgage adviser, were for and indicated that we were going to receive a XXXX percentage point reduction. 

It wasn't until XX/XX/XXXX, more than a week after we went under contract and with less than XXXX weeks before our closing date, that our adviser informed us she had made a mistake. We could not get the promotional rate unless we paid off our current Chase mortgage before closing on our new home. That had never been mentioned to us and our adviser admitted she had failed to disclose that important caveat. 

The XX/XX/XXXX notification came at about XXXXXXXX XXXX after we had gone to bed. And our loan objection deadline in our contract was XXXX XXXX on XX/XX/XXXX. {$75000.00} in XXXX money was on the line. 

We asked that Chase honor its rate commitment to us as we had already taken money out of the stock market to buy the home, lined up renters on our new property, and were in the process of moving existing assets ( XXXX IRA ) to Chase to get an even bigger rate discount. 

Just before XXXX XXXX on XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX wrote back to tell us they would not honor the rate reduction unless we paid off our current Chase mortgage of which there was about {$600000.00} remaining at a 3.125 % rate. 

With less than XXXX weeks before close, there was no way for us to sell our home in that time frame or come up with the rest of the mortgage. 

Chase had the gall to offer us a personal loan to pay off our existing mortgage -- at a much higher rate than our mortgage. 

With just a few hours to spare on our loan objection deadline, we terminated our contract. 

Fortunately, we were able to recoup our XXXX money. However, unfortunately, we are still out the cost of the good faith deposit and our inspection costs, as well as tens of thousands of dollars in taxes that we're going to have to pay on the money we took out of the stock market in anticipation of our purchase. 

We asked Chase to pay back a {$500.00} " good faith '' deposit we had submitted, as well as to reimburse us the {$870.00} for our home inspection. They have not responded to the former and they denied the latter. 

Had we known about this requirement, we XXXX not have decided to pursue a new home purchase at all. We likely wouldn't have locked our rate and we certainly would have shopped around for a better rate than the ones Chase was offering at the time. 

We absolutely wouldn't have made an offer on a {$1.00} XXXX house. And we wouldn't have taken money out the stock market, moved money to Chase to seize relationship pricing and lined up renters on our XXXXXXXX XXXX 

The only person we have had contact with at Chase about this debacle is our lending adviser. We reached out to her manager and never received a response.

In early XXXX, a very large tree fell on my home, along with other storm damage rendering it uninhabitable. My insurance company approved a major claim ( exceeding {$30000.00} ) and issued funds to XXXX Mortgage to endorse and send to me to get the repairs done. For nearly two years, XXXX has withheld these funds while my family has been displaced. 

Initial Out-of-Pocket Costs : I paid nearly {$18000.00} upfront to remove the tree after XXXX promised immediate reimbursement XXXX It took months and months of repeated document requests to be reimbursed. Each time I provided requested documentation, they demanded additional items, each time getting more absurd. At one point, they were requiring the contractor to submit their business bank statements before releasing any funds. 

Unauthorized Repairs and Additional Damage : XXXX hired unlicensed third-party contractors to attempt to perform work, however, most was not covered by the insurance scope. This work was substandard and ultimately caused additional damage to the property. The XXXX party appeared at odd hours in unmarked, damaged vehicles and would abruptly leave when neighbors approached. XXXX then filed a separate vandalism claim and a supplement claim, both of which were approved by the insurance company. However, none of these funds were released to me for repairs, put towards the house for repairs, and would never be explained as to what they did with the money. 

Contractor Coordination Failures : Multiple times, XXXX representatives confirmed funds would be released within days, allowing me to schedule contractors. Each time, they later contradicted this and refused to release funds without explanation or documentation of where the insurance money went. 

Current Financial Hardship : My wife, XXXX XXXX XXXX daughter ( who was a baby when this started ), and I have lived in temporary housing for nearly two years while paying for both rent and the mortgage. To this day, the property continues deteriorating and remains uninhabitable due to incomplete repairs. XXXX continues demanding mortgage payments while withholding the insurance funds needed to make the property habitable, while reminding me about foreclosure if payments arent made. 

Loss Draft Department Issues : Since XXXX, I have documented every interaction with XXXX. The Loss Draft department has been evasive, argumentative, and refuses to provide clear answers about fund disbursement or next steps. At this point, multiple representatives recently admitted no additional funds would be released, while some add they do not see any funds at all. This is the part that continues to baffle me as we are talking large sums of money going missing. 

Pattern of Behavior : I have talked to multiple council, with majority indicating this is a common practice by XXXX. Even my insurance company stated they are familiar with XXXX engaging in this behavior. 

I am requesting investigation into XXXX handling of insurance claim funds and their property preservation practices that have left my home uninhabitable for almost two years while holding large sums in approved insurance funds.

Regarding : XXXX XXXX XXXX, NewRez LLC, and Shellpoint Mortgage Servicing Subject : Ownership Misrepresentation, Governance Collapse, Servicing Violations, and Operation of an Orphaned Servicer Entity 1. Ownership Contradiction : Parent Disclaims Subsidiary While SEC XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX has submitted contradictory statements to federal and state regulators : To XXXX : XXXX stated it is not associated with NewRez/Shellpoint. 
To the XXXX : XXXX certifies, under penalty of securities law, that it owns, controls, and consolidates NewRez/Shellpoint as core subsidiaries and primary income sources. 

Both statements can not be true. 

This contradiction renders : proxy materials, XXXX  certifications, consolidated financials, XXXX  qualification filings, XXXX beneficial ownership, the Form XXXX shelf, the Series E preferred offering, and risk disclosures materially unreliable under federal law. 



2. Legal Consequence : NewRez Becomes an Orphan Entity Under Regulatory Standards If the parent disclaims ownership of the subsidiary, the subsidiary becomes an orphan entity in the eyes of regulators. 

This is the accurate legal concept used in federal oversight. 
Such an entity is not fake, collapsed, or destroyedit is orphaned, meaning : no lawful parent, no lawful governance chain, no validated reporting structure, no clear consolidating entity, no legitimate controller, no validated board oversight, no lawful XXXX  certification pathway, no validated income consolidation. 

This is not theoretical. 
This is the classification used when a parent companys regulatory statements contradict its SEC identity. 



3. NewRez as a Disassociated Subsidiary ( Loss of Controller ) A company must have : a parent, a controller, a governing board, and a financial consolidator. 

If XXXX states we do not own NewRez, then the controller ceases to exist under federal definitions. 

Legally, NewRez becomes : A standalone, unparented servicer operating without a valid controller also known as a disassociated subsidiary. 

Regulators classify these as : high-risk, misreported, potentially non-compliant, and subject to direct supervisory intervention. 



4. Toxic SEC Filings Render NewRezs Financials Unreliable NewRez does not file standalone SEC reports. 
XXXX consolidates : revenue, expenses, servicing income, MSRs, goodwill, liabilities, risk disclosures. 

If the parent filings collapse, then : all of NewRezs financial data becomes unreliable inputs under XXXX XXXX and SEC Rule 12b-20. 

NewRez becomes, in regulatory language : a XXXX box servicer lacking validated controls. 



XXXX. XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX = No Lawful Oversight of NewRez XXXX XXXX : appoints NewRez leadership, authorizes oversight committees, establishes risk policies, ensures XXXX compliance, certifies consolidated reporting. 

If the parents board is void ab initio due to a defective proxy built on false ownership assertions : NewRez has no lawful governing authority above it. 

This creates an entity operating on paper, but with no legitimate supervisory lineage. 

Regulators classify this as : Ungoverned but operating the highest-risk category for a mortgage servicer. 



6. What NewRez Becomes Under This Scenario A servicer operating without : a valid corporate parent, validated financial oversight, lawful XXXX XXXX XXXX, legitimate governance lineage, a functional controller, or validated consolidation. 

In regulatory terms : NewRez becomes an orphaned, unanchored, high-risk servicer with no lawful parent signature authority. 

Not criminal per se but structurally illegitimate, and therefore subject to escalated scrutiny.

7. Enforcement Classification : Free-Floating Regulated Entity In enforcement language, NewRez becomes : a free-floating regulated entity, or a disavowed subsidiary, because : it claims a parent, the parent disclaims it, SEC filings contradict the disclaimer, the parents board is invalid, therefore the controller ceases to exist in law. 

Meaning NewRez now operates with : no validated authority chain, no validated XXXX certifications, no validated reporting, no validated governance, no validated oversight.

8. Strategic Enforcement Consequence Regulators must treat NewRez as : unstable, unanchored, oversight-deficient, structurally compromised, unable to rely on parent filings, and subject to heightened enforcement. 

This strengthens my : RESPA claims, servicing misconduct claims, CFPB filings, XXXX filings, governance-failure arguments, pattern-and-practice evidence, and board-legitimacy challenge.

9. Servicing Misconduct and Ledger Manipulation NewRez has repeatedly issued : contradictory payment histories, disappearing payments, unapplied funds, retroactive adjustments, false escrow numbers, threats based on incorrect data, and recorded admissions that the system is wrong. 

This violates : RESPA ( 12 U.S.C. 2605 ) Reg X ( 12 C.F.R. 1024 ) FDCPA ( 15 U.S.C. 1692 ) state consumer protection laws These failures are not isolated errors they reflect systemic instability.

10. Damages Due to : unlawful accounting, system-wide misrepresentation, emotional and financial harm, reputational injury, and wrongful foreclosure exposure, I seek {>= $1,000,000} in total damages. 



11. Request for Regulatory Action I request a full investigation of : 1. Rithms conflicting ownership claims 2. Validity of XXXX proxy ( XXXX XXXX XXXX ) XXXX. Validity of XXXX certifications 4. On XX/XX/XXXX and XX/XX/XXXX, my mortage company ( Guarenteed ) Rate ( services by XXXX XXXX  charged two {$25.00} fees for when an electronic payments were not processed by my bank. There is a {$50000.00} per day limit that I was unaware of. They waived one of the fees and I paid the other {$25.00} fee and rescheduled the payments to be under the limit.

I am confused why they tried to process {$100000.00} in a single day since they indicate the daily limit must be under this amount.

XXXX XXXX is inaccurately reporting multiple 180 days past due delinquencies on my HELOC ( ending XXXX ), including for months that were covered by a mutually agreed forbearance period. These XXXX delinquencies do not reflect my actual payment history and contradict federal credit reporting rules. 

We entered a forbearance in XXXX. During a forbearance, payments are deferred by agreement. Under the CARES Act XXXX ( amending FCRA XXXX ( a ) ( XXXX ) ( F ) ), any account that was current at the start of a forbearance must be reported as current for the entire duration of the forbearance, and the lender XXXX not report the forbearance months as delinquent. 

Despite this, XXXX XXXX is treating the entire forbearance period as if it were delinquent and is lumping those months into a XXXX delinquency after the forbearance ended. This is not allowed. A missed payment after the forbearance ends XXXX start a new delinquency, but delinquency can not be backdated into the forbearance period. 

In addition, the reporting is inconsistent across all three credit bureaus : TransUnion shows 180 days late in XX/XX/XXXX Experian shows 180 days late in XX/XX/XXXX Equifax shows 180 days late in XX/XX/XXXX The account then shows as current shortly after. It is not mathematically or procedurally possible for an account to be 180 days delinquent in several different months and then immediately current. This clearly indicates incorrect reporting. 

I spoke with a representative ( XXXX XXXX ), who stated that because I did not make a payment immediately when the forbearance ended, the bank considered the entire forbearance period delinquent and aged it into a XXXX late. This is not how delinquency aging works under the FCRA or XXXX guidelines. Delinquency must be aged based on consecutive missed cycles after the accommodation ends. I was never XXXX billing cycles behind after forbearance, so a XXXX delinquency can not be supported. 

I have asked XXXX XXXX to explain how the forbearance months were handled and to provide documentation supporting their reporting method. They have not provided this information and have only repeated general statements that the reporting is accurate, without addressing the specific issue. 

I am requesting : Removal of all inaccurate XXXX delinquencies. 

Correction of all forbearance-covered months to current, as required by XXXX XXXX XXXX. 

Reporting only the actual delinquency that occurred after the forbearance ended ( closer to 3060 days, not XXXX ). 

Consistent reporting across all three bureaus. 

This inaccurate reporting is causing significant credit harm, and I am requesting CFPB assistance to correct it.

This mortgage and escrow account were set up intentionally so that my payments on a monthly basis ( taxes and insurance included ) would be anticipated and collated. I've had this mortgage for going on 11 years now and US Bank continues year after year to incorrectly calculate the amount needed for my escrow account. I believe there's been a shortage in that account EVERY YEAR or almost every year. This year that shortage totaled almost {$2500.00}. 

I called and spoke with someone named XXXX. XXXX agreed the shortage amount was substantial. My question was if my insurance is due in XXXX and my escrow balance is only being reviewed annually in XXXX, WHY wasn't I notified as soon as the shortage became apparent in XXXX of 2024. Why does US Bank only recalculate escrow balances annually? Why would the algorithm or script not see that my insurance payment was substantially larger and then reevaluate the payments? Why allow it to go on for 11 more months and not take steps to notify me of a possible error in the projection? Why is it that even now I know what my next insurance payment is going to be {$4200.00} but US Bank has only accounted for {$3200.00}? Why does US Bank not make a more concerted effort to accurately calculate the needed funds for escrow accounts? XXXX repeatedly asserted that US Bank is only required to calculate escrows annually and that it's based on the state I live in. How does that help at all? Why is there no option to recalculate quarterly or recalculate once a bill has been paid/received which was substantially larger than the projected amount? Why is it that US Bank chooses to calculate the escrow based on outdated numbers and doesn't take into account an insurance bill that's already been made available to them? Even my gas company manages to recalculate quarterly, but US Bank can't. 

US Bank hasn't managed to get this escrow balance calculation right in over 10 years. Why should I tolerate that level of ineptitude and indifference? One would almost think that since US Bank hasn't been able to correctly project an escrow balance in 10 years that they wanted the process to fail.

Specifically, he omitted : his claim that my deferral was never actually approved, only that I was eligible, his insistence that I needed to make XXXX regular monthly payments so they could try again for a deferral, his promise that Valon would advocate for me if I did this and then, maybe I would be granted a deferral and any documentation regarding this alleged investor denial.

Even more concerning : my online Valon account now shows XXXX contradictory payment demands. 

XXXX box shows a full delinquent amount due in XXXX, as if no deferral ever existed. 

The second box shows a trial loan modification payment due in XXXX, which explicitly states that by making that payment, I agree to the trial modification terms even though I never requested or authorized any loan modification.

Valon also previously pressured me to take actions that appear financially beneficial to the servicer, not to the borrower. Now they claim I was merely eligible and that no deferral was ever granted. This retroactive revision of the facts after issuing formal approval and having me sign documents feels intentionally misleading and extremely concerning. 

This conduct feels strategic, not accidental. It appears designed to confuse, mislead, and coerce me into loss-mitigation options that benefit the servicer financially. 

Damages and Harm : I want to reiterate that I am seeking compensation for damages due to : time spent correcting servicer-caused errors emotional distress and financial stress misleading and contradictory information refused payments that worsened the servicer-created delinquency unauthorized changes to my loan status the stress and confusion caused by having an approved deferral canceled without notice the confusion of having XXXX conflicting payment obligations displayed online attempts to steer me into a more expensive and less favorable loan modification Valons actions are not consistent with the behavior of a compliant mortgage servicer. complaint_what_happened:
Dear Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, I am submitting this complaint regarding serious issues and potentially deceptive practices tied to a solar loan I entered into with GoodLeap ( formerly LoanPal ), arranged through the now-defunct XXXX XXXX. I reside in South Carolina and respectfully request your assistance in investigating this matter under the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX and applicable federal consumer protection laws. 

Background : In XX/XX/XXXX, I entered into a solar panel financing agreement with GoodLeap, initiated by XXXX XXXX. I was assured by both the installer and the financier that : My payments would be managed solely through GoodLeap. 

The payment amount and servicing structure were fixed. 

I would receive federal tax refunds not credit and warranty support for 25 years. 

Issues and Concerns : Undisclosed Loan Ownership Transfer : I later learnedonly through my own effortsthat my loan is now allegedly owned by XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX ( XXXX XXXX. Neither XXXX nor GoodLeap ever disclosed this transfer to me. I have repeatedly requested documentation from both entities confirming the loans transfer, but I have received no response. 

No Access or Acknowledgment by XXXX : Although XXXX appears to now own the loan, their representatives deny having any specific loan documentation or servicing knowledge. This leaves me in a difficult position as a consumer, unsure of who truly owns my loan or who is responsible for servicing it. 

Inconsistent Credit Reporting : Despite opening the loan in XX/XX/XXXX, it does not appear on my XXXX report. However, it does show up on my XXXX XXXX app under my XXXX score. This inconsistency raises further questions about reporting and transparency. I also would like to note here that in XXXX of XXXX I reached out to XXXX to discuss the ownership of my loan etc and found out at the end of XXXX that when I connected XXXX they illegally ran and pull my credit report without my permission. 
Servicing Confusion and Lack of Documentation : XXXX continues to process my payments and act as if they own the loan. They refuse to provide any documentation confirming a transfer or showing how my personal and financial data was lawfully shared with XXXX. 

Contractual Discrepancies : On the original XXXX XXXX contract, my husband XXXX is listed as the primary signee. On the GoodLeap documentation, I am listed as the sole borrower. This inconsistency in borrower identity is deeply concerning and potentially invalidates parts of the agreement. 

Void Warranties and XXXX Bankruptcy : XXXX XXXX, the installer, has gone out of business. I am now left without any warranty enforcement or service path. XXXX, the product manufacturer or affiliated entity, has also not clarified their responsibilities in the wake of XXXX  closure. 

Ignored Requests for Information : I have made multiple attempts to contact both GoodLeap and XXXX to obtain my loan documentation and to verify ownership. Both parties have been unresponsive and continue to deflect responsibility. XXXX has also failed to clarify their role in ongoing system support. 

XXXX XXXX Contract - Warranty Violations : XXXX XXXX XXXX 's contract includes a Roof XXXX Warranty ( Section XXXX ), which guarantees weather-tight roof penetrations for 25 years, provided no work is performed by third parties. However, this warranty fails to account for damages caused by XXXX XXXX own installation process or any issues resulting from their poor workmanship. The warranty also contains numerous exclusions, including leaks unrelated to the system, pre-existing roof issues, and damage from foreign objects. This raises concerns about XXXX XXXX accountability for the damage caused during installation. 
Additionally, the XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX ( Section XXXX ) and Damage Warranty ( Section XXXX ) offer limited coverage, with exclusions that XXXX prevent proper repairs or compensation for damages caused during the installation process. These clauses indicate a lack of sufficient consumer protection and raise questions about XXXX XXXX obligations to resolve issues in a fair and timely manner. 

XXXX XXXX and GoodLeap - Breach of Contract and Default : Under Section XXXX of the XXXX XXXX XXXX contract, the company is in default if it fails to perform its obligations within 30 days of receiving written notice ( Section XXXX ). XXXX XXXX is also in default if it admits insolvency or files for bankruptcy. Despite these provisions, XXXX XXXX has failed to meet its contractual obligations, including addressing system defects and providing timely repairs or replacements. As per the contract, the remedies for default ( Section XXXX ) allow the customer to terminate the agreement and pursue legal action. 
Further complicating the situation, GoodLeap, the financing partner for XXXX XXXX, has similarly failed to uphold its own obligations regarding warranty claims, customer service, and support. This failure to perform and the lack of accountability from both XXXX XXXX and XXXX not only constitutes breach of contract but also reflects deceptive practices. The combination of these defaults and unresolved issues, including poor workmanship, broken warranties, and financial irresponsibility, warrants the termination of the agreements and pursuit of all available legal remedies. 
Application of XXXX XXXX of XXXX : XXXX XXXX acted as the originating party for my solar panel loan and made material misrepresentations on behalf of GoodLeap, including misleading claims about tax benefits, payment terms, and warranties. XXXX presented XXXX financing as part of a bundled offering, conducted the loan application process, and submitted my information directly to GoodLeap. 

Because XXXX XXXX acted as an agent or authorized partner of XXXX  their loan product to customers and benefiting XXXX by securing loan originationsGoodLeap XXXX be legally responsible for XXXX actions under the agency theory of liability. The fact that XXXX is now defunct does not absolve GoodLeap of its duty to honor the terms and representations made by its agent. 

GoodLeap profited from the deal and is still processing payments, which further supports the argument that they were in a principal-agent relationship with XXXX. Therefore, they should be held accountable for any fraudulent misrepresentations and for the loss of warranty coverage and service. 

Broader Pattern of Misconduct : My experience appears to be part of a larger pattern of deceptive practices by GoodLeap : Minnesota Attorney General Lawsuit : In XX/XX/XXXX, the Minnesota Attorney General filed a lawsuit against GoodLeap and other solar lenders, alleging violations of consumer-fraud statutes, including deceptive lending practices and failure to disclose material terms. 

In further support of my concerns, Id like to reference a lawsuit filed on XX/XX/XXXX, by Minnesota Attorney General XXXX XXXX against GoodLeap and XXXX other solar lenders. The lawsuit alleges that these companies engaged in deceptive lending practices, including hiding substantial dealer fees ( as high as XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX  ) within the loan principal, which consumers were never made aware of. These hidden fees artificially inflated loan balances and interest charges, often without any corresponding explanation to the borrower. The Attorney General asserts that such conduct violates XXXX consumer fraud, deceptive trade practices, and usury laws. The lawsuit reflects a broader pattern of misconduct consistent with my own experience including a lack of transparency, misleading loan terms, and improper disclosures. 
( Source : Minnesota XXXX Official Site ) Arbitration Cases : GoodLeap has lost key arbitration cases where consumers were misled about loan terms and solar system performance. In a recent arbitration handled by XXXX XXXX XXXX, GoodLeap was found liable for the misconduct of its partner company, XXXX XXXX. The arbitrator ruled that XXXX extensive involvement with XXXX XXXX constituted an agency relationship, holding them responsible for deceptive sales practices, including misrepresentations about cost savings and damage caused during installation. The client was awarded approximately {$13000.00} in damages, had a {$90000.00} loan canceled, and received attorneys fees. This case sets a strong precedent for other consumers misled by similar solar financing arrangements. ( Goodleap XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX ) Consumer Complaints : Numerous consumers have reported similar issues, including unauthorized loan transfers, lack of transparency, and defective solar installations. 

Supporting Evidence XXXX Involvement in Prior Credit Reporting Misconduct : In addition to my personal experience, I want to highlight that GoodLeap ( formerly LoanPal ) was recently the subject of a class action settlement involving improper credit reporting practices tied to XXXX XXXX, another defunct solar installer. According to the official XXXX, consumers alleged that GoodLeap reported solar loans as delinquent or charged off, even when installations were incomplete, systems were defective, or disputes were ongoing. 

This resulted in significant credit damage to consumers in circumstances strikingly similar to mine. GoodLeap ultimately agreed to resolve these claims, demonstrating a documented pattern of misconduct, negligence, and disregard for fair credit reporting standardsparticularly when partnered installers go out of business, like XXXX XXXX in my case. I also XXXX be entitled to this settlement on the grounds that my financing was through Goodleap. 

XXXX XXXX : XXXX XXXX, et al. v. XXXX XXXXXXXX XXXX, XXXX Settlement

complaint_what_happened:
I am the surviving borrower on a mortgage serviced by Mr. Cooper. My husband, a co-borrower, is deceased. I requested removal of the deceased borrower from the loan. Mr. Cooper has demanded that I complete full underwriting and pay approximately {$3200.00} in fees to do so. 

I repeatedly requested the specific investor or federal guideline that requires full underwriting and fees solely to remove a deceased borrower when the surviving borrower is already on title and has made all payments. 

Mr. Cooper did not provide any guideline supporting this requirement. Instead, they cited a generic investor document that is unrelated to the removal of a deceased borrower and does not mandate underwriting or fees in this situation. 

When I pointed out that the cited document was unrelated and again requested the correct guideline, Mr. Cooper stated that they would no longer communicate with me because they had already explained their position, despite never providing the requested documentation. 

Mr. Cooper is now refusing to engage further while continuing to impose a requirement they have not substantiated. This constitutes stonewalling and misrepresentation of investor requirements.

In XX/XX/XXXX, a mortgage application was submitted to XXXX XXXX for an FHA loan. Since that time, the underwriting process has been repeatedly delayed and is now at risk of denial despite all requested documentation being provided in good faith. 

Social Security Overpayment Concern In late XX/XX/XXXX, underwriting raised concerns regarding a Social Security overpayment connected to qualifying income. 

The following documentation was provided : Social Security benefit verification showing full monthly benefits continue SSA correspondence confirming no active collection or withholding Bank statements showing full benefit deposits with no reductions confirmation that no repayment plan exists proof that a refund was issued The Social Security Administration confirmed the review is administrative and there is no timeline for completion. They also confirmed no recovery action is occurring and benefits continue in full. 

The obligation was also included in an active bankruptcy case, meaning it is not legally collectible outside the bankruptcy process. 

Despite this documentation, the lender continued requesting a Letter of Resolution that the Social Security Administration can not provide until its internal review process is completed. 

Rent Verification Issue In early XX/XX/XXXX, the landlord submitted a verification of rent form that contained wording suggesting a late payment. 

After reviewing the form, the landlord confirmed the wording was inaccurate and submitted a corrected verification stating : no 30-day late payments positive rental history The landlord also contacted the lender directly to clarify the misunderstanding. Although the corrected form resolved the issue, the matter continued to be raised. 

Repeated Requests & Lack of Clear Guidance Between XX/XX/XXXX and XX/XX/XXXX : documentation was repeatedly requested after submission requirements appeared to change without clear guideline references requests were made for documents that government agencies can not provide on demand clear written guidance identifying what would satisfy underwriting requirements was not provided communication delays occurred while deadlines approached Impact The prolonged delays and shifting documentation requests have placed the mortgage approval at risk despite good faith compliance and submission of all available documentation. 

Requested Resolution I respectfully request : 1. A compliance review of the underwriting process 2. A clear written explanation citing the specific FHA guideline preventing approval 3. confirmation whether the submitted documentation satisfies underwriting requirements 4. assurance that underwriting standards are being applied consistently and fairly All requested documentation has been provided, and we are seeking a transparent and fair resolution.

My mortgage was originated by XXXX  XXXX  XXXX  on XX/XX/year> and is currently serviced by RoundPoint Mortgage Servicing. The fully executed Promissory Note from closing shows three borrowers : myself ( XXXX XXXX ), my wife ( XXXX XXXX ), and my brother in law ( XXXX XXXX XXXX ). RoundPoint has provided inconsistent and incorrect information about our loan for several days. Multiple RoundPoint representatives told me that only two borrowers were on the loan and that my brother in law was not part of the mortgage. After four calls, RoundPoint finally acknowledged that all three of us are in fact on the loan and that all three of us had a late payment reported to the credit bureaus.

RoundPoint also had the wrong phone number on file. This prevented proper delinquency notifications from reaching us. Neither my wife nor I received any valid notice prior to the reported late payment. My brother in law did not receive any notice either. Because all three of us are borrowers, the servicer had a responsibility to notify all parties, and this did not happen.

The failure to notify us, the incorrect borrower information provided repeatedly, and the confusion inside RoundPoint created a servicing error that directly led to the negative credit reporting. The balance was paid immediately once we learned of the issue.

I am requesting that CFPB review this matter, and that RoundPoint remove or suppress the derogatory credit reporting for all borrowers because the late reporting resulted from servicing errors, incorrect borrower handling, and failure to notify all responsible parties.

I have documentation including the signed Promissory Note that confirms all three borrowers. I am attaching that with this complaint.

complaint_what_happened: This all started back in XX/XX/XXXX. We received a letter from TD Bank and our mortgage had gone up about {$1000.00}. We called to see why and they explained to us at our escrow account needing more money and thats why the payment went up. This was due to the fact that our income tax on our home was listed wrong and it was a lot more and thats why the escrow was taking a lot more out. We had filed with our local county tax office that this was our primary residence, but they never adjusted that. We got that fixed, but they couldnt backdate the month of the mortgage so it was still significantly higher than what we could afford. I was Im XXXX XXXX and was actually not getting paid so we didnt have the financial funds to cover that amount. They told me they could do a loan modification to help with this Process, so we filled out all the paperwork and sent it back in a timely manner that they requested at the end of XXXX XXXX months would go by and I do see XXXX here and there saying it was still being processed, but no other information was given to me because it wasnt the department that was processing it. It was just customer service. I kept calling to ask what was taking so long they couldnt really give me an answer why, then fast forward to XX/XX/XXXX I receive more paperwork to sign for the loan modification. I kept trying to call back and said I dont wan na sign another agreement because I dont understand why I have to re-sign one one Ive already signed one and no one can tell me why Im resigning. Go to find out they said I was missing a witness signature which I wasnt because I had a copy of the documentation that I sent back and no signatures were missing. So then they sent me more paperwork because it now is outdated to sign all over again. I re-sign the paperwork sent it back in a timely matter. Then another 30+ days goes by and theyre still processing it. About 2 to 3 months later I receive more paperwork to sign. They couldnt really give me a reason why Im signing more sent that back within 30 days. This process went on for a couple more months. Now were almost at the two year mark from when this whole started and I have signed seven different agreements and sent them back in a time of matter. I have large customer complaints and Ive heard from that department but at the end of the day no ones giving me the right answers. No ones helping me out. And now I learned that the last agreement that we sent back, I will be getting more paperwork something was missing that they shouldve saw in the beginning of this whole process. This whole process has been very stressful and very frustrating because my credit has taken such a nice significant hit because TD Bank is showing late payments on my mortgage because they have not been applying any payment that I have made to the amount that we owe. I just dont know what else to do. Maybe go to a lawyer, but that would be spending money. I dont have in time and energy and like I said Im very stressed. I just need someone to help me and no one seems to be wanting to help me in any of this.

sub_issue: Trying to communicate with the company to fix an issue while managing or servicing your loan
complaint_what_happened:
I took out a {$15000.00} home improvement loan with Goodleap that had a promotional period ending XX/XX/XXXX. I overpaid by {$15000.00} before XX/XX/XXXX, and I have not received my refund or an update as of submitting this on XX/XX/XXXX. 
On XX/XX/XXXX I called Goodleap. I requested and received a payoff quote. Shortly after, a physical check for the full balance was mailed to Goodleap in accordance with the payoff quote. 
On XX/XX/XXXX, I called Goodleap and I was informed that they had not received any payments for my account. I requestedthat they check their mail for the payment I made. The customer service representative advised me that they could not, and I was assured that any payments would be posted online within hours of receipt.I asked what would happen if I sent an electronic payment in addition to the check they said they hadn't received yet, and I was told I would receive a refund for the overpaid amount in the same form it was sent. I don't remember the exact timeframe they gave for the refund, but it was less than a month.And so on XX/XX/XXXXXXXX  I sent two electronic payments totaling {$15000.00} to pay off the full balance of the loan before the promotional period ended. I received email receipts from Goodleap for both of these payments on XX/XX/XXXX. 
On XX/XX/XXXXXXXX  I received an email with a bill showing I had nothing due on the loan. 
On XX/XX/XXXXXXXX  I checked my account and saw that the initial check payment had posted to my account effective XX/XX/XXXX, prior to the XX/XX/XXXXXXXX  electronic payments. I called Goodleap and spoke to another customer service representative. I requested and received a paid-in-full letter, which included the following : '' We have mailed you a check if we owe you money. If we received more than we needed to pay off the account, we have sent a check or credited your funding source on file. You should receive the payment within 21 days of the payoff date. " I asked for more clarification about the refund, and I was told that each of the two electronic payments would be refunded electronically, each to the bank account it came from. I was told that refunds typically take 7-15 days.I also voiced my displeasure that I was told on XXXX that Goodleap had not received the check they now state was received on XXXX, which prompted me to unnecessarily send them an overpayment of {$15000.00}. Additionally, the payment evidently was not visible on the account within hours of receipt, as I had been assured earlier. 
On XX/XX/XXXXXXXX  I called Goodleap to ask about the status of my refund, since it had been longer than 21 days since I got the document saying I should receive the payment within 21 days of the payoff date. The customer service representative confirmed that I was indeed owed a refund. But I was informed that the refund was not set to be processed, and that they had just submitted the refund for processing and I should wait to receive the refund. I asked again if the refund would be electronic and returned to the bank accounts that each of the two payments came from, and I was told that was correct. I asked to be sent email updates on my refund, but I never received anything. 
On XX/XX/XXXX I called Goodleap to ask about the status of my refund, and I was told no progress had been made. It was confirmed that I am owed a refund. My call was escalated to a supervisor, who told me that it's taking longer than the specified timeframe because the amount I'm owed is so large. I was assured that the refund would be processed. I asked for a timeframe, and I was told it would probably be within the week. I repeated my request for email updates on my refund, but I never received anything. 
On XX/XX/XXXX I called Goodleap and requested that my call be escalated. I spoke with another supervisor who told me my refund was still waiting to be processed. I asked if there was another escalation level, and I was told the supervisor had done so, which gives the department in charge of refunds 24-48 hours to respond. I was told I would get an email or a phone call when an update comes in. I verified that they have the correct email and phone number to contact me. I was informed on this call that I would not receive an electronic refund, but due to the large amount of the refund I would be sent a physical check through the mail. I voiced my displeasure that this is contrary to what I had been told multiple times before. I asked if the check could be sent with tracking to ensure I receive it, but I was told no. I asked if the check could be sent via overnight or express shipping, but I was told no. I asked if the refund could be sent electronically instead, but I was told no. I asked what I should do if I didn't hear or receive anything, and I was told to call back and ask for an update. I voiced my displeasure at the situation and requested that my displeasure be passed along. I asked for and received confirmation that if I don't receive an update by XXXX on XX/XX/XXXX ( XXXX hours after the second escalation ) that I should call back. 
On XX/XX/XXXXXXXX  I submitted an online complaint with the CFPB, since I had not received an email or phone call by the deadline Goodleap gave.

I was aware that I needed to be current on said mortgage, so I called the day after I made my payment for XXXX. As part of this call, the customer service rep guaranteed me the next payment due would be XX/XX/XXXX. XXXX, since the XXXX payment was already made. Can't the mortgage company determine on their end that the payment went through? So many customer service issue here, one of which might be illegal.

I am filing this complaint regarding the improper assessment of legal and late fees on a mortgage loan serviced by Shellpoint Mortgage Servicing XXXX Newrez LLC ) for the property located at XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX 

Following the passing of my late brother, I assumed responsibility for managing the loan and made timely payments while working to obtain the legal documentation required to become the estate administrator. During this period, Shellpoint assessed multiple legal and late fees that I believe were improperly charged, given the circumstances. The total disputed amount is approximately {$2400.00}, including legal fees and late charges that accrued while I was resolving estate matters. 

I first contacted Shellpoint regarding these charges on XX/XX/XXXX, requesting a thorough review of my account history from XX/XX/XXXX onward, when the loan was transferred to them. I provided documentation, including my late brothers death certificate and proof of my role as estate administrator, to support my request. 

After three months of waiting, Shellpoint responded on XX/XX/XXXX, but their response failed to address my request. Instead of conducting an investigation or providing an explanation for the fees, they simply sent me a billing history, which did not clarify why these charges were assessed or justify their validity. 

I followed up immediately, restating my concerns and providing a detailed breakdown of the specific fees I was disputing. Despite this, Shellpoints subsequent response dismissed my concerns outright, stating that my inquiry was the same as a previous one ( which is incorrect ) and that they would not conduct an additional investigation. They did not review the documentation I provided, nor did they explain the fees in question. 

I find Shellpoints handling of this matter deeply unprofessional. Their lack of transparency, failure to conduct a proper review, and months-long delay in responding to a bereavement-related issue are unacceptable. I have exhausted all reasonable efforts to resolve this matter directly with the company, and I am now seeking CFPB intervention to ensure a fair and thorough investigation into these disputed charges. 

There also significant complaints regarding NewRez on multiple sites stating and further providing evidence that this is how they run their business and have no intent on rectifying their broken customer service processes. 

Requested Resolution : A full investigation into the legal and late fees assessed on my account. 
A clear explanation, with documentation, of why each charge was applied and under what terms of the loan agreement. 
A reimbursement of any fees that were improperly charged. 

Key Dates & Actions Taken : XX/XX/XXXX Submitted initial request to Shellpoint regarding disputed fees. 
XX/XX/XXXX Received a response with only a billing history, no investigation conducted. 
XX/XX/XXXX Followed up with additional documentation outlining the specific charges in question. 
XX/XX/XXXX Received a dismissive response stating they would not investigate further without reviewing my provided information. 
XX/XX/XXXX - Followed up requesting the information again and reiterated the additional documentation I provided. 
Amount in Dispute : ~ {$2400.00} Supporting Documents : Payment history highlighting disputed fees. 
Death certificate and estate administrator documentation. 
Copies of correspondence with Shellpoint.

complaint_what_happened:
I have an FHA-insured mortgage with Newrez/ shellpoint mortgage. In XX/XX/XXXX, my co-borrower was in an accident, resulting in a significant loss of household income due to lost wages. As a result, we fell behind on our mortgage. As of XX/XX/XXXX, the loan is one payment delinquent and will be two payments delinquent by the end of the week. 

I submitted a hardship assistance application to my mortgage servicer, clearly documenting the income loss and requesting assistance to avoid further delinquency. The servicer denied my application, stating that I am not eligible for assistance until the account is at least 91 days delinquent. 

This policy directly contradicts FHA servicing guidelines and HUD Mortgagee Letter requirements, which mandate that servicers evaluate borrowers for available loss mitigation options as soon as they are aware of a hardshipnot only after reaching 90+ days delinquency. FHA guidelines require that servicers make every reasonable effort to help the borrower avoid default, including reviewing for a repayment plan, forbearance, or modification, even if the delinquency is under 90 days. 

By refusing to review my hardship application solely because I am not yet 91 days past due, the servicer is failing to comply with HUD loss mitigation requirements, CFPB mortgage servicing rules ( Regulation X, 12 C.F.R. 1024.41 ), and their duty to engage in early intervention under 12 C.F.R. 1024.39. 

I have been proactive in seeking assistance to prevent further delinquency. The servicers refusal to act unless I fall further behind is not only harmful but also appears to be in violation of federal servicing standards for FHA loans. 

I am requesting that the CFPB require my servicer to : 1. Immediately review my hardship application under applicable FHA and federal servicing guidelines.

2. Offer all available loss mitigation options to help avoid further delinquency and potential foreclosure.

My mortgage servicing was transferred from my prior servicer to Fay Servicing effective XX/XX/XXXX ( as stated in Fay Servicings own correspondence ). I had sufficient funds ready since XX/XX/XXXX to pay off my mortgage in full and was simply waiting for the new servicers payoff quote. 

The prior servicer emailed me on XX/XX/XXXX, to inform me of the upcoming transfer date. However, Fay Servicing did not send me any welcome letter or account information until it mailed a Welcome Letter dated XX/XX/XXXX, from Texas. I did not receive this Welcome Letter until late XXXX and it arrived together in the same mail delivery as a XX/XX/XXXX statement and delinquency/late fee notice dated XX/XX/XXXX. 

Both the XX/XX/XXXX statement and the XX/XX/XXXX delinquency notice were generated before Fay Servicing was even the official servicer ( effective date XX/XX/XXXX ). Sending a delinquency/late fee notice within 60 days of a servicing transfer also appears to violate RESPA ( XXXX XXXX. XXXX ( d ) ). This timing strongly suggests that the mailing of these critical documents was delayed or batched, depriving me of any meaningful opportunity to make my XXXX payment or request a payoff earlier in the month. Had I received the Welcome Letter when it was dated ( XX/XX/XXXX ) or earlier, I could have acted immediately and avoided any XXXX interest. 

As soon as I received the Welcome Letter and account number, I created my Fay Servicing online account and submitted my first payoff request on XX/XX/XXXX the earliest possible date given their delayed mailing. However, Fay Servicing did not respond to my payoff request at all until I called on XX/XX/XXXX, to follow up. Only after my call did a representative finally email me the payoff quote. 

When I pointed out that this delay was entirely on their side and that I should not be charged interest beyond my requested payoff date, the representative instead warned me that late fees could be charged if I did not pay soon. This was not only unhelpful but also misleading, given that RESPA prohibits assessing late fees or reporting delinquency within 60 days after a servicing transfer. 

Despite these facts, Fay Servicing is attempting to charge me interest for the full months of both XXXX and XXXX. 

Charging interest for XXXX is unreasonable because the sole reason I could not pay off the loan in early XXXX was Fay Servicings failure to provide timely account information and payoff access. 

Charging interest for the full month of XXXX is also improper. Under the XXXX XXXX rule ( TILA Regulation Z XXXX ( c ) ( XXXX ) ), full month interest charges on residential mortgage payoffs are prohibited for loans originated after XX/XX/XXXX ; my loan originated in XX/XX/XXXX, so only per diem interest through my XX/XX/XXXX request date should apply. 

The combination of : - Delayed and untimely mailing of required account information ( Welcome Letter dated XX/XX/XXXX but received late XXXX, together with XX/XX/XXXX statement and delinquency notice ), - Issuing billing and delinquency notices before the effective transfer date of XX/XX/XXXX, - Failure to respond to a timely payoff request until XXXX days later, - Sending an improper delinquency notice within the XXXX protection period, - Attempting to impose XXXX interest caused solely by their own delay, and - Attempting to impose prohibited full month interest for XXXX, has caused me significant and unnecessary financial harm, despite my good faith efforts to pay off the loan as soon as possible.

Company : Fifth Third Bank Product : Mortgage Issue : Mortgage servicing, escrow mismanagement, forceplaced insurance, successorininterest violations What happened : I am filing this complaint because Fifth Third Bank severely mishandled my deceased fathers mortgage account, caused a massive and unjustified escrow deficit, failed to pay required insurance, forceplaced extremely expensive insurance without proper notice, and failed to properly recognize me as the legal successor in interest. Their actions have caused significant financial harm to me and my family. 
My father passed away, and Fifth Third had his death certificate and documentation showing that I am the legal successor in interest. Despite this, Fifth Third failed to properly manage the escrow account and failed to pay the homeowners insurance from escrow. As a result, the insurance company canceled the policy. 
Instead of notifying me or correcting their error, Fifth Third forceplaced insurance on the property. Forceplaced insurance is extremely expensive, and Fifth Third charged these costs to the escrow account without proper notice and without giving me an opportunity to provide proof of insurance. This caused the escrow account to become severely negative approximately {$17000.00} which is unreasonable and appears to be the result of Fifth Thirds own servicing failures. 
I believe Fifth Third violated multiple federal mortgage servicing rules, including : Failure to pay escrowed homeowners insurance Failure to send required forceplaced insurance notices ( 45day, 30day, and final notice ) Failure to recognize and communicate with me as a confirmed successor in interest under Regulation X Failure to provide accurate escrow accounting Failure to manage the loan in good faith after the borrowers death Failure to transfer accurate servicing records to the new servicer ( Shellpoint ) Fifth Third then transferred the loan to XXXX with a massive escrow deficit and without correcting any of the errors they caused. XXXX is now sending lossmitigation packets addressed to my deceased father, even though they have the death certificate and successorininterest documentation. This is causing confusion and preventing me from resolving the account. 
I am requesting that the CFPB investigate Fifth Third Bank for : 1. Mishandling escrow funds 2. Failing to pay insurance premiums 3. Wrongfully forceplacing insurance 4. Failing to send required notices 5. Failing to properly recognize a successor in interest 6. Transferring a damaged loan to a new servicer without correcting errors 7. Causing a massive and unjustified escrow shortage What I want : I want Fifth Third to provide a full escrow history, proof of all notices sent, proof of all insurance payments, and an explanation for why they failed to pay the insurance. I want the wrongful forceplaced insurance charges removed or refunded, and I want the escrow shortage corrected. I also want the successorininterest status properly recognized and communicated to the new servicer. 
Fifth Thirds actions have caused serious financial harm and emotional distress to my family, and I am requesting the CFPBs assistance in resolving these issues.

To Whom This May Concern, I am writing to formally express concern regarding a significant escrow and property tax billing issue for which we were not at fault, yet are now being required to bear substantial financial responsibility. 

We purchased and moved into the property on XXXX XXXX and have consistently made all mortgage and escrow payments as billed since that time. 

On XX/XX/XXXX, we received an annual escrow statement from our mortgage lender, LoanCare, indicating an escrow surplus of {$950.00} for which we were issued a refund. Approximately two weeks later, we received another annual escrow statement reflecting an escrow shortage of {$10000.00}. Upon calling the mortgage lender, we were informed that they had been billing the incorrect parcel ID number for the last XXXX years. As a result of the error being corrected, the mortgage company determined that we owed over {$10000.00} in back taxes. 

Throughout this period, we consistently made all mortgage and escrow payments as billed by LoanCare and relied on the accuracy of the information provided. Based on the information we were given, LoanCare paid property taxes each year according to the amounts billed by the XXXX XXXX. It appears this error originated with the Taxing Authoritys use of an incorrect parcel ID rather than any action on our part. 

The discrepancies for each year are as follows : In XXXX our lender paid our tax bill of {$2000.00}, but they should have paid {$4000.00}. 
In XXXX our lender paid our tax bill of {$1600.00}, but they should have paid {$3600.00}. 
In XXXX our lender paid our tax bill of {$1500.00}, but they should have paid {$3200.00}. 
Totals {$5100.00} {$10000.00} The XXXX XXXX refunded the lender {$2000.00} in XXXX, {$1600.00} in XXXX, and {$1500.00} in XXXX, totaling {$5100.00}, which LoanCare credited to our escrow account. As a result, we received escrow refund checks in the amount of {$1300.00} in XXXX and {$950.00} in XXXX. Based on the corrected information, we should not have received these refunds. 

Additionally, it is our understanding that the property tax payments made for tax years XXXX were applied to an incorrect parcel identification number and were subsequently refunded to the lender once the error was identified. These refunded payments were supposedly credited to our escrow account. 

Based on this information, the actual escrow shortage related to the corrected tax payments should be {$7400.00}, not the {$10000.00} currently being assessed. This indicates a discrepancy of {$3400.00}, which remains unaccounted for at this time. 
We respectfully request a detailed escrow analysis and accounting to explain how the total shortage of {$10000.00} was calculated and to identify where the missing {$3400.00} was applied. 

On XX/XX/XXXX, LoanCare identified the incorrect parcel ID and subsequently paid {$10000.00} to the XXXX XXXX to satisfy the unpaid taxes for the three-year period. Because the original payments had been refunded, our account was considered delinquent for tax years XXXX until this payment was made. We have since confirmed with the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX that all taxes are now paid in full and no additional amounts are owed. 

Despite this, we are now being required to repay the full escrow shortage resulting from this error. As we are unable to pay more than {$10000.00} in a single payment, the shortage has been spread over 24 months, increasing our monthly mortgage payment by over {$500.00}. This creates a significant financial burden stemming from an error outside of our control. 

We believe this issue should have been identified much earlier, and we respectfully request that responsibility be reviewed by the appropriate parties. We are seeking prompt communication regarding potential remedies, adjustments, or accountability related to this matter.

complaint_what_happened:
In XX/XX/2025, I entered into a 4-month repayment plan with Rocket Mortgage to cure a delinquency on my mortgage loan. The approval letter dated XX/XX/2025, stated that my past-due amount was {$5500.00} and noted that the past-due amount may include unpaid principal, interest, escrow, late charges, and advances. The letter also stated that completion of the repayment plan would bring the loan current. 

The repayment plan required monthly payments of {$3600.00} for four months, consisting of my regular monthly payment of {$2400.00} plus an additional {$1200.00} toward the delinquency. I successfully completed all required payments under the plan. 

After completing the plan, I discovered that {$650.00} in late fees remained outstanding on the loan. I contacted Rocket Mortgage and was informed that although the past-due amount at the time was {$5500.00} ( which included late fees ), the repayment plan was internally calculated using only {$4900.00} ( two monthly payments of {$2400.00} ), and that the {$650.00} in late fees were intentionally excluded from the plan. I was told that these late fees would remain on the loan balance until paid. 

At no point in the approval letter or during discussions with representatives was I informed that a different amount was being used to calculate the repayment plan than the stated past-due balance, or that late fees tied to the stated delinquency would remain on the account after successful completion of the plan. The letter indicated the past-due amount may include late charges and represented that completion of the plan would bring the loan current. 

I requested clarification, escalation to management, and a goodwill waiver of the late fees due to this disclosure discrepancy. Rocket Mortgage acknowledged confusion but declined to waive the {$650.00}. 

My concern is that the borrower-facing approval letter disclosed one past-due amount while the repayment plan was calculated using a different amount without disclosure. I relied on the written approval letter when entering and completing the plan.

Company : Rocket Mortgage State : Pennsylvania Complaint : Rocket Mortgage previously corrected my escrow going forward after my prior CFPB complaint, but my monthly payment remains inflated because improper charges caused by servicing errors are still included in my escrow balance and shortage calculation. 

In Rocket Mortgages prior written response, the company admitted that a servicing error occurred when it failed to properly contact my homeowners insurance provider during the policy cancellation and renewal period. This servicing failure triggered the lender-placed insurance process and resulted in an insurance lapse charge of approximately {$470.00}, which was assessed to my loan and included in escrow calculations. 

Additionally, penalties were charged in connection with supplemental/interim property tax bills and paid through escrow, further increasing the escrow shortage and my monthly payment. Even if base taxes were my responsibility, penalties caused by billing and payment handling should not be passed to the borrower when servicing failures contribute to the charges. 

Although Rocket Mortgage adjusted my future escrow estimate, it has not removed the improper insurance lapse charge or tax penalties from my escrow account balance. As a result, my monthly payment is still approximately {$240.00} higher than it should be and I am being required to repay a shortage that was caused by improper servicing-related charges. 

What I am requesting : Removal and reimbursement of lender-placed insurance lapse charges caused by servicing error Removal of tax penalties paid through escrow that contributed to the escrow shortage Recalculation of escrow account after improper charges are removed Adjustment of my monthly payment to the correct amount Written documentation confirming the corrections Correcting future escrow projections does not resolve improper past charges that continue to inflate my payment and escrow shortage.
